title: Plain-language biomarker descriptions
description: Plain-language educational descriptions for every canonical biomarker in lib/canonical-biomarkers.json, identity-keyed by EXACT canonical name. Each entry carries an optional abbreviation, a full_name expansion, and a 1-3 sentence description of what the marker measures and why it generally matters. INFORMATIONAL, NOT MEDICAL ADVICE — general educational content, not personal interpretation, dosing, or diagnosis. A coverage test (lib/__tests__/biomarker-descriptions.test.ts) fails the build if a canonical biomarker is added without a description here.

citation.source: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia (U.S. National Library of Medicine)
citation.note: General consumer-health reference for lab-test / biomarker educational content.

citation.source: Testing.com (formerly Lab Tests Online; ADLM / AACC)
citation.note: General reference for lab-test explanations. Descriptions here are curated original plain-language summaries — informational, not medical advice.

entries.name: Total Cholesterol
entries.description: Total cholesterol is the combined amount of all cholesterol carried in the blood, including LDL, HDL, and other lipoproteins. It gives a broad snapshot of blood lipids, though the individual fractions matter more; higher levels are generally associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: LDL Cholesterol
entries.description: LDL cholesterol is carried by low-density lipoproteins and is the main type that can build up in artery walls. Elevated LDL is one of the strongest lipid-related risk factors for heart disease, which is why it is often called "bad" cholesterol.

entries.name: HDL Cholesterol
entries.description: HDL cholesterol is carried by high-density lipoproteins, which help remove excess cholesterol from tissues and return it to the liver. Higher HDL is generally associated with lower cardiovascular risk, so it is often called "good" cholesterol.

entries.name: Triglycerides
entries.description: Triglycerides are the most common type of fat circulating in the blood, used for energy storage and transport. Elevated fasting triglycerides are associated with metabolic problems and higher cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: VLDL Cholesterol
entries.description: VLDL cholesterol is carried by very-low-density lipoproteins, which mainly transport triglycerides from the liver. It is usually estimated from triglyceride levels, and higher values are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
entries.description: Non-HDL cholesterol is total cholesterol minus HDL, capturing all the cholesterol carried by potentially artery-clogging particles. It is considered a strong overall marker of cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: ApoB
entries.description: Apolipoprotein B is a protein found on LDL and other atherogenic particles, with one molecule per particle, so it reflects the total number of cholesterol-carrying particles that can enter artery walls. Higher ApoB is closely linked to cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: Lipoprotein(a)
entries.description: Lipoprotein(a) is an LDL-like particle whose blood level is largely determined by genetics. Elevated levels are an independent risk factor for heart disease and stroke.

entries.name: Cholesterol/HDL Ratio
entries.description: This ratio compares total cholesterol to HDL cholesterol and is used as a summary measure of lipid-related cardiovascular risk. Lower ratios are generally considered more favorable.

entries.name: Glucose
entries.description: Glucose is the main sugar in the blood and the body's primary immediate energy source. Fasting glucose is used to screen for and monitor diabetes and blood-sugar regulation; persistently high levels are associated with diabetes.

entries.name: Hemoglobin A1c
entries.description: Hemoglobin A1c reflects the average blood glucose over roughly the past two to three months by measuring the fraction of hemoglobin with glucose attached. It is a standard test for diagnosing and monitoring diabetes.

entries.name: Insulin
entries.description: Insulin is the hormone that allows cells to take up glucose from the blood. Fasting insulin levels help assess insulin resistance; elevated levels can indicate the body is working harder to keep blood sugar controlled.

entries.name: HOMA-IR
entries.full_name: Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance
entries.description: HOMA-IR is a calculated index that combines fasting glucose and fasting insulin to estimate insulin resistance. Higher values suggest the body's cells are responding less effectively to insulin.

entries.name: C-Peptide
entries.description: C-peptide is released in equal amounts to insulin when the pancreas makes insulin, so it serves as a marker of how much insulin the body is producing. It helps distinguish between different causes of blood-sugar problems.

entries.name: hs-CRP
entries.description: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein is a marker of low-grade inflammation in the body, measured with a sensitive assay. Elevated levels are associated with increased cardiovascular risk and active inflammation.

entries.name: Homocysteine
entries.description: Homocysteine is an amino acid in the blood that is processed with the help of B vitamins such as B12, B6, and folate. Elevated levels can indicate a vitamin deficiency and are associated with cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: Uric Acid
entries.description: Uric acid is a waste product formed when the body breaks down purines from food and cells. High levels can crystallize in joints and are associated with gout and kidney stones.

entries.name: GGT
entries.description: GGT is an enzyme found mainly in the liver and bile ducts. Elevated levels can indicate liver or bile-duct issues and are often associated with alcohol use.

entries.name: ALT
entries.description: ALT is an enzyme concentrated in liver cells that is released into the blood when they are damaged. It is a widely used marker of liver health, with elevations suggesting liver stress or injury.

entries.name: AST
entries.description: AST is an enzyme found in the liver, heart, and muscles that rises when those tissues are damaged. It is commonly measured alongside ALT to assess liver health.

entries.name: Alkaline Phosphatase
entries.description: Alkaline phosphatase is an enzyme found mainly in the liver, bile ducts, and bone. Elevated levels can point to liver or bile-duct problems or to increased bone activity.

entries.name: Total Bilirubin
entries.description: Bilirubin is a yellow pigment produced when the body breaks down old red blood cells and is processed by the liver. Elevated total bilirubin can cause jaundice and may indicate liver or bile-duct issues or increased red-cell breakdown.

entries.name: Albumin
entries.description: Albumin is the most abundant protein in blood, made by the liver, that helps maintain fluid balance and transports many substances. Low levels can reflect liver disease, kidney loss, or poor nutrition.

entries.name: Total Protein
entries.description: Total protein measures the combined amount of albumin and globulins in the blood. It gives a general picture of nutrition, liver and kidney function, and immune activity.

entries.name: Sodium
entries.description: Sodium is an electrolyte that regulates fluid balance, nerve function, and blood pressure. Levels outside the normal range can reflect hydration status or kidney and hormonal issues.

entries.name: Potassium
entries.description: Potassium is an electrolyte essential for nerve signaling, muscle contraction, and normal heart rhythm. Both high and low levels can affect the heart, so it is carefully monitored.

entries.name: Chloride
entries.description: Chloride is an electrolyte that works with sodium to maintain fluid balance and the body's acid-base status. Abnormal levels often accompany hydration or acid-base disturbances.

entries.name: Carbon Dioxide
entries.description: This test measures the amount of carbon dioxide, mostly as bicarbonate, in the blood and reflects the body's acid-base balance. Abnormal levels can indicate metabolic or respiratory problems.

entries.name: Calcium
entries.description: Calcium in the blood is essential for bones, nerve signaling, muscle function, and blood clotting. Levels are tightly regulated by hormones, and abnormal values can reflect parathyroid, bone, kidney, or vitamin D issues.

entries.name: Magnesium
entries.description: Magnesium is a mineral involved in hundreds of enzyme reactions, including muscle and nerve function, blood sugar control, and energy production. Low levels can cause muscle cramps and affect heart rhythm.

entries.name: Phosphorus
entries.description: Phosphorus is a mineral that works with calcium to build bones and is central to energy metabolism. Levels are regulated by the kidneys and hormones, and abnormal values can reflect kidney or parathyroid issues.

entries.name: BUN
entries.description: Blood urea nitrogen measures the amount of urea, a waste product of protein breakdown, that the kidneys filter from the blood. It is used to assess kidney function and hydration.

entries.name: Creatinine
entries.description: Creatinine is a waste product from normal muscle activity that the kidneys filter out of the blood. Blood levels are a key indicator of kidney function, with higher levels suggesting reduced filtering.

entries.name: eGFR
entries.description: eGFR is a calculated estimate of how much blood the kidneys filter each minute, based on creatinine along with age and sex. It is the main measure used to stage kidney function, with lower values indicating reduced function.

entries.name: PhenoAge
entries.description: PhenoAge is a calculated 'biological age' in years, estimated from nine routine blood analytes (albumin, creatinine, glucose, hs-CRP, lymphocyte percentage, MCV, RDW, alkaline phosphatase, and white blood cell count) together with chronological age, using a published mortality-risk model. It is interpreted relative to your actual age: a PhenoAge below your chronological age suggests biologically younger physiology. It is a population-level estimate with an error of several years, not a precise measurement, and is informational rather than diagnostic.

entries.name: Cystatin C
entries.description: Cystatin C is a protein filtered by the kidneys that is used as an alternative marker of kidney function, sometimes more reliable than creatinine. Higher levels suggest reduced kidney filtration.

entries.name: Hemoglobin
entries.description: Hemoglobin is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the body. Low levels indicate anemia, while high levels can reflect dehydration or other conditions.

entries.name: Hematocrit
entries.description: Hematocrit is the proportion of blood volume made up of red blood cells. Low values suggest anemia and high values can indicate dehydration or increased red-cell production.

entries.name: White Blood Cell Count
entries.description: The white blood cell count measures the number of immune cells in the blood that defend against infection. Elevated counts often signal infection or inflammation, while low counts can reflect immune suppression.

entries.name: Red Blood Cell Count
entries.description: The red blood cell count measures the number of oxygen-carrying cells in the blood. Abnormal counts can indicate anemia, dehydration, or increased red-cell production.

entries.name: Platelet Count
entries.description: Platelets are small blood cells that clump together to form clots and stop bleeding. Low counts can increase bleeding risk, while high counts can increase clotting risk.

entries.name: MCV
entries.description: MCV measures the average size of red blood cells. It helps classify types of anemia, with small cells often seen in iron deficiency and large cells in B12 or folate deficiency.

entries.name: RDW
entries.description: RDW measures how much red blood cells vary in size. A higher RDW means greater size variation and can be an early clue to certain anemias or nutritional deficiencies.

entries.name: Neutrophils
entries.description: Neutrophils are the most common type of white blood cell and the body's first responders to bacterial infection. Elevated levels often indicate infection or inflammation.

entries.name: Lymphocytes
entries.description: Lymphocytes are white blood cells, including T and B cells, that drive the immune system's targeted defenses. Changes in their numbers can reflect viral infections, immune conditions, or immune suppression.

entries.name: Ferritin
entries.description: Ferritin is a protein that stores iron, and its blood level reflects the body's iron reserves. Low ferritin indicates depleted iron stores, while high ferritin can reflect inflammation or iron overload.

entries.name: Iron
entries.description: Serum iron measures the amount of iron circulating in the blood bound to transport proteins. It is interpreted alongside other iron tests to assess iron deficiency or overload.

entries.name: TIBC
entries.description: TIBC measures the blood's total capacity to bind and transport iron, reflecting the amount of transferrin available. It rises in iron deficiency and helps evaluate iron status.

entries.name: Transferrin Saturation
entries.description: Transferrin saturation is the percentage of iron-transport protein that is actually carrying iron. It helps assess iron status, being low in deficiency and high in iron overload.

entries.name: TSH
entries.description: TSH is a pituitary hormone that signals the thyroid to make thyroid hormones. It is the primary screening test for thyroid function, with high levels suggesting an underactive thyroid and low levels an overactive one.

entries.name: Free T4
entries.description: Free T4 measures the unbound, active form of the thyroid hormone thyroxine available to tissues. It is used with TSH to assess thyroid function.

entries.name: Free T3
entries.description: Free T3 measures the unbound, active form of triiodothyronine, the more potent thyroid hormone. It helps evaluate thyroid function, particularly in overactive-thyroid conditions.

entries.name: Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy
entries.description: This is the main storage form of vitamin D and the best measure of overall vitamin D status. Adequate levels support bone health, immune function, and calcium balance; low levels indicate deficiency.

entries.name: Vitamin B12
entries.description: Vitamin B12 is essential for red blood cell formation, nerve function, and DNA synthesis. Deficiency can cause anemia and neurological symptoms.

entries.name: Folate
entries.description: Folate is a B vitamin needed for making DNA and red blood cells and is especially important in pregnancy. Deficiency can cause a type of anemia.

entries.name: Testosterone, Total
entries.description: Total testosterone measures all the testosterone in the blood, both bound and free. It is the main sex hormone in men and also important in women, influencing muscle, bone, libido, and energy.

entries.name: Testosterone, Free
entries.description: Free testosterone is the small fraction of testosterone not bound to proteins and available for the body to use. It can give a more accurate picture of active hormone levels than total testosterone alone.

entries.name: DHEA-Sulfate
entries.description: DHEA-sulfate is a hormone made mainly by the adrenal glands and serves as a building block for other sex hormones. Levels naturally decline with age and can reflect adrenal function.

entries.name: Cortisol
entries.description: Cortisol is the body's main stress hormone, made by the adrenal glands, and it regulates metabolism, blood sugar, and the stress response. Levels follow a daily rhythm, and abnormal values can indicate adrenal or pituitary problems.

entries.name: IGF-1
entries.description: IGF-1 is a hormone produced mainly by the liver in response to growth hormone and mediates many of its growth-promoting effects. It is used to assess growth hormone activity.

entries.name: PSA
entries.description: PSA is a protein made by the prostate gland that circulates in the blood. It is used to screen for and monitor prostate conditions, with elevated levels warranting further evaluation.

entries.name: Blood Pressure Systolic
entries.description: Systolic blood pressure is the higher number in a reading, measuring the pressure in the arteries when the heart beats. Persistently high values indicate hypertension, a major cardiovascular risk factor.

entries.name: Blood Pressure Diastolic
entries.description: Diastolic blood pressure is the lower number in a reading, measuring arterial pressure when the heart rests between beats. Elevated values contribute to the diagnosis of high blood pressure.

entries.name: Oxygen Saturation
entries.description: Oxygen saturation is the percentage of hemoglobin in the blood that is carrying oxygen, often measured with a fingertip sensor. Low values indicate the blood is not carrying enough oxygen.

entries.name: Respiratory Rate
entries.description: Respiratory rate is the number of breaths taken per minute. It is a basic vital sign, and abnormal rates can signal respiratory, cardiac, or metabolic problems.

entries.name: Resting Heart Rate
entries.description: Resting heart rate is the number of heartbeats per minute while at rest. A lower resting rate often reflects better cardiovascular fitness, while a persistently high rate can be a sign of stress or reduced fitness.

entries.name: Body Temperature
entries.description: Body temperature reflects the balance between the heat the body produces and loses. Fever or an abnormally low temperature can signal infection or other conditions.

entries.name: Body Fat Percentage
entries.description: Body fat percentage is the proportion of total body weight that is fat tissue. It offers a more complete picture of body composition than weight alone.

entries.name: Bone Mineral Density T-Score
entries.description: The bone mineral density T-score compares a person's bone density to that of a healthy young adult. Lower scores indicate weaker bones and higher risk of osteoporosis and fractures.

entries.name: Visceral Adipose Tissue
entries.description: Visceral adipose tissue is the fat stored deep in the abdomen around internal organs. Higher amounts are more strongly linked to metabolic and cardiovascular risk than fat stored just under the skin.

entries.name: Appendicular Lean Mass Index
entries.description: The appendicular lean mass index estimates muscle mass in the arms and legs relative to height. It is used to assess muscle adequacy, with low values associated with reduced strength and frailty.

entries.name: VO2 Max
entries.description: VO2 max is the maximum rate at which the body can use oxygen during intense exercise and is a key measure of cardiorespiratory fitness. Higher values reflect greater aerobic capacity and are associated with better long-term health.

entries.name: Grip Strength
entries.description: Grip strength is the maximum force of a handgrip measured with a dynamometer, used as a simple proxy for whole-body muscle strength. Lower grip strength for one's age and sex is a well-studied predictor of frailty and mortality, so the trend matters more than any single reading.

entries.name: 30-Second Chair Stand
entries.description: The 30-second chair stand counts how many times a person can rise from and return to a seated position in 30 seconds without using their arms. It gauges lower-body strength and endurance and is a standard measure of functional fitness and fall risk in older adults.

entries.name: Single-Leg Balance
entries.description: Single-leg balance measures how long a person can stand on one leg, typically with eyes open. It reflects postural control and is associated with fall risk; the ability generally declines with age, so tracking it over time is more informative than a one-off value.

entries.name: Alternaria Alternata (M6) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies specific to Alternaria alternata, a common outdoor mold. Elevated levels indicate sensitization and a possible allergy to this mold, which can trigger respiratory symptoms.

entries.name: Aspergillus Fumigatus (M3) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to Aspergillus fumigatus, a widespread mold found indoors and outdoors. Elevated levels indicate sensitization that can contribute to allergic respiratory symptoms.

entries.name: Bermuda Grass (G2) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to Bermuda grass pollen, a common cause of seasonal grass-pollen allergy. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this grass.

entries.name: Birch (T3) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to birch tree pollen, a frequent cause of springtime allergies. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to birch pollen.

entries.name: Cat Dander (E1) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to cat dander. Elevated levels indicate sensitization and a possible allergy to cats.

entries.name: Cladosporium Herbarum (M2) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to Cladosporium herbarum, one of the most common outdoor molds. Elevated levels indicate sensitization that can trigger respiratory allergy symptoms.

entries.name: Cockroach (I6) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to cockroach allergens, a common indoor trigger especially in urban environments. Elevated levels indicate sensitization associated with asthma and allergy symptoms.

entries.name: Common Ragweed (Short) (W1) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to short ragweed pollen, a leading cause of late-summer and fall hay fever. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to ragweed.

entries.name: Cottonwood (T14) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to cottonwood tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this common springtime tree pollen.

entries.name: Dermatophagoides Farinae (D2) IgE
entries.full_name: American House Dust Mite (D2) IgE Antibody
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to the American house dust mite (Dermatophagoides farinae). Elevated levels indicate sensitization to dust mites, a common year-round indoor allergy trigger.

entries.name: Dermatophagoides Pteronyssinus (D1) IgE
entries.full_name: European House Dust Mite (D1) IgE Antibody
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to the European house dust mite (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus). Elevated levels indicate sensitization to dust mites, a common indoor allergen.

entries.name: Dog Dander (E5) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to dog dander. Elevated levels indicate sensitization and a possible allergy to dogs.

entries.name: Elm (T8) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to elm tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this tree pollen.

entries.name: Maple (Box Elder) (T1) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to maple and box elder tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this common tree pollen.

entries.name: Mountain Cedar (T6) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to mountain cedar pollen, a major cause of winter allergy in some regions. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to cedar pollen.

entries.name: Mouse Urine Proteins (E72) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to proteins found in mouse urine, an indoor allergen relevant in some homes and workplaces. Elevated levels indicate sensitization.

entries.name: Mugwort (W6) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to mugwort pollen, a common weed that causes fall allergy symptoms. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to mugwort.

entries.name: Oak (T7) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to oak tree pollen, a common spring allergen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to oak pollen.

entries.name: Penicillium Notatum (M1) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to Penicillium notatum, a common indoor and outdoor mold. Elevated levels indicate mold sensitization that can contribute to respiratory allergy symptoms, and are distinct from a penicillin drug allergy.

entries.name: Rough Pigweed (W14) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to rough pigweed pollen, a weed that contributes to seasonal allergy. Elevated levels indicate sensitization.

entries.name: Sheep Sorrel (W18) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to sheep sorrel pollen, a weed allergen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization.

entries.name: Sycamore (T11) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to sycamore tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this tree pollen.

entries.name: Timothy Grass (G6) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to timothy grass pollen, one of the most common causes of grass-pollen allergy. Elevated levels indicate sensitization.

entries.name: Walnut Tree (T10) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to walnut tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this tree pollen.

entries.name: White Ash (T15) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to white ash tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this spring tree pollen.

entries.name: White Mulberry (T70) IgE
entries.description: This test measures IgE antibodies to white mulberry tree pollen. Elevated levels indicate sensitization to this tree pollen.

entries.name: PFAS - Branched PFOA Isomers
entries.description: This measures branched isomers of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), one of the "forever chemicals" that accumulate in the body. Higher blood levels reflect greater environmental exposure and are being studied for potential health effects.

entries.name: PFAS - Branched PFOS Isomers
entries.description: This measures branched isomers of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), a persistent "forever chemical." Higher levels reflect greater cumulative environmental exposure.

entries.name: PFAS - Linear PFOA Isomers
entries.description: This measures the linear form of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), a long-lasting industrial chemical. Higher blood levels reflect greater exposure to this contaminant.

entries.name: PFAS - Linear PFOS Isomers
entries.description: This measures the linear form of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), a persistent environmental pollutant. Higher levels indicate greater cumulative exposure.

entries.name: PFAS - MeFOSAA
entries.description: This measures MeFOSAA, a PFAS compound that forms as other "forever chemicals" break down. Its level reflects exposure to this class of persistent contaminants.

entries.name: PFAS - PFDA
entries.description: This measures perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA), a long-chain PFAS "forever chemical." Higher levels reflect greater environmental exposure.

entries.name: PFAS - PFHxS
entries.description: This measures perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), a persistent PFAS compound. Higher blood levels indicate greater cumulative exposure.

entries.name: PFAS - PFNA
entries.description: This measures perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), a long-chain PFAS "forever chemical." Its level reflects exposure to this class of contaminants.

entries.name: PFAS - PFUnDA
entries.description: This measures perfluoroundecanoic acid (PFUnDA), a long-chain PFAS compound. Higher levels reflect greater environmental exposure.

entries.name: PFAS - NASEM Recommended Summation
entries.description: This is the sum of several PFAS compounds as recommended by the National Academies (NASEM) to gauge total PFAS body burden. It is used to interpret overall exposure and guide follow-up.

entries.name: Aluminum
entries.description: Aluminum is a metal the body does not need; blood testing checks for elevated exposure. High levels can be a concern, particularly for people with reduced kidney function.

entries.name: Arsenic
entries.description: Arsenic is a toxic element found in some water, food, and industrial sources. Elevated blood or urine levels indicate recent exposure, which over time can affect multiple organ systems.

entries.name: Lead
entries.description: Lead is a toxic heavy metal with no safe level; testing measures recent and ongoing exposure. Elevated levels can affect the nervous system, kidneys, and blood, and are especially harmful to children.

entries.name: Mercury
entries.description: Mercury is a toxic metal encountered through sources such as certain fish and industrial exposure. Elevated levels can affect the nervous system and kidneys.

entries.name: Chromium
entries.description: Chromium is a trace element; small amounts play a role in metabolism, but testing usually screens for excess exposure, such as from certain metal implants. Very high levels can indicate unwanted exposure.

entries.name: Molybdenum
entries.description: Molybdenum is an essential trace mineral that supports several enzymes. Testing can assess deficiency or excess, though imbalances are uncommon.

entries.name: Copper
entries.description: Copper is an essential trace mineral needed for iron metabolism, connective tissue, and nerve function. Both deficiency and excess can cause health problems.

entries.name: Selenium
entries.description: Selenium is an essential trace mineral with antioxidant functions and a role in thyroid metabolism. Both low and high levels can affect health.

entries.name: Zinc
entries.description: Zinc is an essential mineral important for immune function, wound healing, and enzyme activity. Deficiency can impair immunity and healing.

entries.name: Iodine
entries.description: Iodine is an essential mineral the thyroid uses to make thyroid hormones. Both deficiency and excess can disturb thyroid function.

entries.name: Amylase
entries.description: Amylase is an enzyme, made mainly by the pancreas and salivary glands, that helps digest carbohydrates. Elevated blood levels often indicate pancreatic inflammation.

entries.name: Lipase
entries.description: Lipase is a digestive enzyme made by the pancreas that breaks down fats. Elevated blood levels are a sensitive marker of pancreatic inflammation.

entries.name: Globulin
entries.description: Globulins are a group of blood proteins that include antibodies and transport proteins. Levels are interpreted alongside albumin to assess immune activity, liver function, and inflammation.

entries.name: Albumin/Globulin Ratio
entries.description: This ratio compares albumin to globulin proteins in the blood. An abnormal ratio can point to liver disease, kidney problems, immune conditions, or inflammation.

entries.name: Basophils
entries.description: Basophils are the least common type of white blood cell and are involved in allergic and inflammatory responses. Elevated levels can occur with allergies or certain blood conditions.

entries.name: Eosinophils
entries.description: Eosinophils are white blood cells that respond to parasites and allergic reactions. Elevated levels often accompany allergies, asthma, or parasitic infections.

entries.name: Monocytes
entries.description: Monocytes are white blood cells that engulf pathogens and debris and help coordinate immune responses. Elevated levels can accompany infection, inflammation, or recovery from illness.

entries.name: MCH
entries.description: MCH measures the average amount of hemoglobin in each red blood cell. It is used with other red-cell indices to classify types of anemia.

entries.name: MCHC
entries.description: MCHC measures the average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells. It helps characterize anemias and distinguish among their causes.

entries.name: MPV
entries.description: MPV measures the average size of platelets. Larger platelets tend to be younger and more active, and the value can offer clues about platelet production and turnover.

entries.name: Magnesium, RBC
entries.description: This test measures magnesium inside red blood cells, which some consider a better reflection of the body's magnesium stores than the standard serum test. Low levels suggest depleted magnesium.

entries.name: Folate, RBC
entries.description: This measures folate stored within red blood cells and reflects longer-term folate status than serum folate. Low levels indicate folate deficiency.

entries.name: Methylmalonic Acid (MMA)
entries.description: Methylmalonic acid accumulates when vitamin B12 is lacking, making it a sensitive marker of B12 deficiency. Elevated levels can reveal a deficiency even when B12 blood levels look borderline.

entries.name: Sex Hormone Binding Globulin (SHBG)
entries.description: Sex hormone binding globulin is a protein that binds sex hormones such as testosterone and estrogen, controlling how much is freely available. Its level affects the interpretation of hormone tests.

entries.name: Prolactin
entries.description: Prolactin is a pituitary hormone best known for stimulating milk production. Elevated levels outside of pregnancy or breastfeeding can affect menstrual cycles, fertility, and libido.

entries.name: Thyroglobulin Antibodies (TgAb)
entries.description: These are antibodies against thyroglobulin, a protein in the thyroid. Their presence can indicate autoimmune thyroid disease.

entries.name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies (TPOAb)
entries.description: These are antibodies against thyroid peroxidase, an enzyme in the thyroid. Elevated levels are a marker of autoimmune thyroid conditions such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

entries.name: Rheumatoid Factor (RF)
entries.description: Rheumatoid factor is an antibody found in the blood of many people with rheumatoid arthritis and some other autoimmune or inflammatory conditions. A positive result supports, but does not by itself confirm, such a diagnosis.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin E (Total)
entries.description: Total IgE measures the overall level of immunoglobulin E, the antibody class involved in allergic reactions. Elevated levels can reflect allergies or parasitic infection.

entries.name: Vitamin A (Retinol)
entries.description: Vitamin A, measured as retinol, is a fat-soluble vitamin important for vision, immune function, and skin health. Both deficiency and excess can cause problems.

entries.name: Vitamin E (Alpha-Tocopherol)
entries.description: Alpha-tocopherol is the main active form of vitamin E, a fat-soluble antioxidant that protects cells from damage. Low levels are uncommon but can affect nerve and muscle health.

entries.name: Vitamin E (Beta/Gamma-Tocopherol)
entries.description: These are additional forms of vitamin E measured alongside alpha-tocopherol. They contribute to the body's antioxidant defenses and give a fuller picture of vitamin E status.

entries.name: Coenzyme Q10
entries.description: Coenzyme Q10 is a compound cells use to produce energy and that also acts as an antioxidant. Levels can be reduced by age and by certain medications.

entries.name: Omega-3 Total (OmegaCheck)
entries.description: This measures the total level of omega-3 fatty acids in the blood. Higher levels are generally associated with cardiovascular and overall health benefits.

entries.name: Omega-3 DHA
entries.description: DHA is an omega-3 fatty acid especially important for the brain, eyes, and nervous system. It is obtained mainly from fatty fish and reflects dietary omega-3 intake.

entries.name: Omega-3 DPA
entries.description: DPA is an omega-3 fatty acid that serves as an intermediate between EPA and DHA. It contributes to the body's overall omega-3 status.

entries.name: Omega-3 EPA
entries.description: EPA is an omega-3 fatty acid with anti-inflammatory properties, obtained mainly from fish. Higher levels are associated with cardiovascular benefits.

entries.name: Omega-6 Arachidonic Acid
entries.description: Arachidonic acid is an omega-6 fatty acid involved in inflammation and cell signaling. Its balance relative to omega-3 fatty acids is often considered when assessing inflammation.

entries.name: Omega-6 Linoleic Acid
entries.description: Linoleic acid is the most common omega-6 fatty acid in the diet and an essential fat the body cannot make. It is measured as part of the overall fatty-acid balance.

entries.name: Omega-6/Omega-3 Ratio
entries.description: This ratio compares omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acids in the blood. A lower ratio is often considered more favorable for inflammation and cardiovascular health.

entries.name: Arachidonic Acid/EPA Ratio
entries.description: This ratio compares the omega-6 fatty acid arachidonic acid to the omega-3 EPA and is used as a marker of the body's inflammatory balance. Lower ratios are generally considered more favorable.

entries.name: LDL Particle Number
entries.description: LDL particle number counts the actual number of LDL particles rather than the cholesterol they carry. Because particle number can differ from LDL cholesterol, it offers an additional view of cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: LDL Small
entries.description: This measures small, dense LDL particles, which are considered more likely to penetrate artery walls than larger ones. Higher levels are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: LDL Medium
entries.description: This measures medium-sized LDL particles as part of a detailed lipoprotein analysis. It contributes to a fuller picture of cardiovascular risk beyond standard cholesterol.

entries.name: LDL Peak Size
entries.description: This measures the predominant size of LDL particles. Larger, more buoyant particles are generally considered less atherogenic than smaller, denser ones.

entries.name: LDL Pattern
entries.description: LDL pattern classifies whether a person's LDL particles are predominantly large (pattern A) or small and dense (pattern B). Pattern B is associated with higher cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: HDL Large
entries.description: This measures large HDL particles, which are thought to be particularly effective at removing cholesterol from tissues. It adds detail to standard HDL cholesterol.

entries.name: Prostate Specific Antigen (PSA), Free %
entries.description: This measures the percentage of PSA in the blood that circulates unbound. It is used alongside total PSA to help refine prostate risk assessment.

entries.name: Urine pH
entries.description: Urine pH measures how acidic or alkaline the urine is. It can reflect diet, hydration, and kidney function and is relevant to kidney-stone and metabolic assessment.

entries.name: Urine Specific Gravity
entries.description: Urine specific gravity measures how concentrated the urine is compared with water. It reflects hydration status and the kidneys' ability to concentrate urine.

entries.name: Red Blood Cells, Urine
entries.description: This test detects red blood cells in the urine. Their presence can indicate urinary-tract irritation, infection, stones, or other conditions warranting evaluation.

entries.name: White Blood Cells, Urine
entries.description: This test detects white blood cells in the urine, which often signal infection or inflammation in the urinary tract.

entries.name: Squamous Epithelial Cells, Urine
entries.description: This measures squamous epithelial cells in the urine, which usually come from the skin or urethra. High numbers often suggest the sample was contaminated during collection.

entries.name: Biological Age
entries.description: Biological age is an estimate of how old the body seems based on health biomarkers, rather than years since birth. A biological age lower than chronological age is generally considered favorable.

entries.name: Neutrophils, Absolute
entries.description: This is the actual number of neutrophils, the main infection-fighting white cells, per volume of blood. Low counts increase infection risk, while high counts often signal infection or inflammation.

entries.name: Lymphocytes, Absolute
entries.description: This is the actual number of lymphocytes, the immune cells that drive targeted defenses, per volume of blood. Abnormal counts can reflect infection, immune conditions, or immune suppression.

entries.name: Monocytes, Relative
entries.description: This is the percentage of white blood cells that are monocytes. It is interpreted alongside the absolute count to assess immune status.

entries.name: Eosinophils, Relative
entries.description: This is the percentage of white blood cells that are eosinophils. Elevated percentages can accompany allergies, asthma, or parasitic infections.

entries.name: Basophils, Relative
entries.description: This is the percentage of white blood cells that are basophils. It is interpreted with the absolute count and can be elevated in allergic or certain blood conditions.

entries.name: Total T4
entries.description: Total T4 measures all thyroxine in the blood, both bound and free. It is one of the tests used to evaluate thyroid function.

entries.name: Total T3
entries.description: Total T3 measures all triiodothyronine in the blood. It is used, often with other thyroid tests, to evaluate thyroid function, particularly overactive-thyroid states.

entries.name: Direct Bilirubin
entries.description: Direct bilirubin is the water-soluble, liver-processed form of bilirubin. Elevated levels point toward problems with the liver or bile ducts.

entries.name: Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)
entries.description: Lactate dehydrogenase is an enzyme found in many tissues and released when cells are damaged. Elevated levels are a nonspecific marker of tissue injury or cell turnover.

entries.name: Creatine Kinase (CK)
entries.description: Creatine kinase is an enzyme found mainly in muscle, including the heart, that is released when muscle is damaged. Elevated levels can follow intense exercise, muscle injury, or heart muscle damage.

entries.name: Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR)
entries.description: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate measures how quickly red blood cells settle in a tube, which increases with inflammation. It is a nonspecific marker of inflammation in the body.

entries.name: Anion Gap
entries.description: The anion gap is a value calculated from electrolyte results that reflects the balance of charged particles in the blood. An abnormal gap can indicate acid-base disturbances.

entries.name: Reticulocytes
entries.description: Reticulocytes are newly made, immature red blood cells. Their level shows how actively the bone marrow is producing red cells, which helps evaluate anemia.

entries.name: Reticulocytes, Absolute
entries.description: This is the actual number of young red blood cells per volume of blood. It indicates how well the bone marrow is responding to the body's need for red cells.

entries.name: Estradiol
entries.description: Estradiol is the main form of estrogen and an important sex hormone in both women and men. It influences the menstrual cycle, bone health, and reproductive function.

entries.name: Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH)
entries.description: Follicle-stimulating hormone is a pituitary hormone that regulates reproductive processes, including egg and sperm development. Its level helps assess fertility and menopausal status.

entries.name: Luteinizing Hormone (LH)
entries.description: Luteinizing hormone is a pituitary hormone that triggers ovulation in women and testosterone production in men. It is used to evaluate reproductive and hormonal function.

entries.name: Triglyceride/HDL Ratio
entries.description: This ratio divides triglycerides by HDL cholesterol (both in mg/dL) and is used as a simple surrogate marker of insulin resistance and small, dense LDL particles. Lower ratios are generally considered more favorable; it is calculated from the lipid panel rather than measured directly.

entries.name: Nucleated Red Blood Cells
entries.description: This is the fraction of immature, still-nucleated red blood cells among white cells (reported per 100 white blood cells). They are normal in newborns but essentially absent in healthy children and adults, where they can signal marrow stress, severe anemia, or low oxygen.

entries.name: Nucleated Red Blood Cells, Absolute
entries.full_name: Absolute Nucleated Red Blood Cell Count
entries.description: This is the actual number of nucleated (immature) red blood cells per volume of blood. Normally near zero beyond the newborn period, a rise can accompany marrow stress, hemolysis, or hypoxia.

entries.name: Immature Granulocytes
entries.description: This is the percentage of early, not-yet-mature granulocytes (such as metamyelocytes and myelocytes) among white blood cells. A rise, called a left shift, often reflects infection, inflammation, or increased marrow activity.

entries.name: Immature Granulocytes, Absolute
entries.description: This is the actual number of immature granulocytes per volume of blood. It is normally very low, and an increase (a left shift) commonly points to infection, inflammation, or marrow stimulation.

entries.name: Hemoglobin A
entries.description: Hemoglobin A is the main form of hemoglobin in adults, measured as a fraction of total hemoglobin by electrophoresis. A reduced proportion can indicate an inherited hemoglobin variant or disorder such as sickle cell disease or thalassemia.

entries.name: Hemoglobin A2
entries.description: Hemoglobin A2 is a minor hemoglobin fraction measured on electrophoresis. An elevated level is a classic marker of beta-thalassemia trait, while a low level can accompany iron deficiency or other thalassemias.

entries.name: Hemoglobin F
entries.description: Hemoglobin F is the fetal form of hemoglobin, dominant at birth and normally falling to very low levels in adults. Persistently high adult levels can occur in hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin and in some thalassemias and hemoglobinopathies.

entries.name: ABO Blood Group
entries.description: The ABO group classifies blood as type A, B, AB, or O based on the antigens carried on red blood cells. It is fixed for life and, with the Rh factor, determines which blood a person can safely receive in a transfusion.

entries.name: Rh Type
entries.description: The Rh factor records whether red blood cells carry the Rh(D) antigen — positive if present, negative if absent. It is fixed for life and pairs with the ABO group to give the full blood type; it also matters in pregnancy, where an Rh-negative parent carrying an Rh-positive fetus may need Rh immune globulin.

entries.name: Blood Type
entries.description: The full blood type combines the ABO group (A, B, AB, or O) with the Rh factor (positive or negative) — for example O positive. It is fixed for life and determines which blood can be safely transfused.

entries.name: Protein, Urine
entries.description: Urine dipstick protein screens for protein leaking into the urine, an early sign of kidney involvement. It is reported qualitatively (Negative, Trace, 1+, and so on), where Negative is normal, and is separate from serum total protein.

entries.name: Glucose, Urine
entries.description: Urine dipstick glucose is normally absent; it appears when blood sugar rises above the level the kidneys can reabsorb. It is reported qualitatively (Negative is normal) and is distinct from a blood glucose measurement.

entries.name: Ketones, Urine
entries.description: Urine ketones are byproducts of fat breakdown, raised during fasting, low-carbohydrate diets, or poorly controlled diabetes. The dipstick result is qualitative, with Negative being normal.

entries.name: Bilirubin, Urine
entries.description: Bilirubin is normally absent from urine; its presence can point to liver or bile-duct disease. The dipstick result is qualitative (Negative is normal) and is separate from serum bilirubin.

entries.name: Blood, Urine
entries.description: The urine dipstick blood pad detects hemoglobin or myoglobin. A positive result prompts a look for red blood cells and possible causes of bleeding; it is qualitative (Negative is normal) and differs from a microscopic red-cell count.

entries.name: Nitrite, Urine
entries.description: A positive urine nitrite suggests bacteria that convert dietary nitrate to nitrite — a clue to a urinary-tract infection. The result is qualitative, with Negative being normal.

entries.name: Leukocyte Esterase, Urine
entries.description: Leukocyte esterase is an enzyme released by white blood cells; a positive urine result signals inflammation or infection in the urinary tract. It is reported qualitatively, with Negative being normal.

entries.name: Urobilinogen, Urine
entries.description: Urobilinogen forms from bilirubin in the gut and a small amount normally appears in urine. Higher levels can reflect liver disease or red-cell breakdown, while its absence can indicate blocked bile flow.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin G
entries.description: IgG is the most abundant antibody class and carries long-term (adaptive) immunity. Low levels can indicate an immunodeficiency; high levels chronic infection or inflammation.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin A
entries.description: IgA is the antibody class that protects mucosal surfaces such as the gut and airways. Selective IgA deficiency is the most common inherited immunodeficiency and is often harmless.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin M
entries.description: IgM is the first antibody produced when the body meets a new infection, so a rise can point to a recent or active exposure.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 1
entries.description: IgG1 is the largest of the four IgG subclasses and responds mainly to protein antigens. Subclass levels are measured when investigating a suspected antibody deficiency.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 2
entries.description: IgG2 responds mainly to the polysaccharide coats of encapsulated bacteria. A selective deficiency can raise susceptibility to those infections.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 3
entries.description: IgG3 is active against protein and viral antigens and has the shortest half-life of the four IgG subclasses.

entries.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 4
entries.description: IgG4 is the least abundant IgG subclass. Markedly elevated levels are associated with IgG4-related disease, a condition that can affect several organs.

entries.name: Leptin
entries.description: Leptin is a hormone released by fat tissue that signals the brain about the body's energy stores and helps regulate appetite. Levels rise with body-fat mass and vary by sex.

entries.name: Albumin, Urine
entries.description: Urine albumin (microalbumin) detects small amounts of the protein albumin leaking into the urine, an early sign of kidney damage. It is usually interpreted as a ratio to urine creatinine.

entries.name: Creatinine, Urine
entries.description: Urine creatinine reflects how concentrated the urine is and is mainly used to standardize other urine measurements, such as the albumin-to-creatinine ratio.

entries.name: Alpha-Fetoprotein (AFP)
entries.description: Alpha-fetoprotein is a protein used as a tumor marker for liver and germ-cell cancers, and as a screening marker in pregnancy. In non-pregnant adults it is normally low.

entries.name: Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA)
entries.description: CEA is a tumor marker used mainly to monitor colorectal and some other cancers over time. Mild elevations can also occur with smoking and some non-cancerous conditions.

entries.name: Hepatitis B Surface Antigen (HBsAg)
entries.description: HBsAg is a marker of active hepatitis B infection. A negative result is normal; a positive result indicates current infection and warrants follow-up.

entries.name: Hepatitis B Surface Antibody (HBsAb)
entries.description: HBsAb (anti-HBs) reflects immunity to hepatitis B from vaccination or recovery from past infection. A level of 10 mIU/mL or higher is generally considered protective.

entries.name: Hepatitis C Antibody (Anti-HCV)
entries.description: The hepatitis C antibody test screens for exposure to the hepatitis C virus. A non-reactive result is normal; a reactive result is followed by confirmatory HCV RNA testing.

entries.name: BUN/Creatinine Ratio
entries.description: The ratio of blood urea nitrogen to creatinine helps distinguish causes of kidney impairment and gives a clue to hydration status. It is a calculated value with no units.

entries.name: LDL/HDL Ratio
entries.description: The ratio of LDL to HDL cholesterol is a summary measure of cardiovascular risk, where a lower ratio is more favorable. It is a calculated value with no units.

entries.name: Intraocular Pressure
entries.description: The fluid pressure inside the eye, measured by tonometry in millimeters of mercury (mmHg). A normal reading is roughly 10-21 mmHg; a pressure above 21 is called ocular hypertension and is a risk factor for glaucoma, though it is not a diagnosis on its own. This entry is used when the report does not say which eye.

entries.name: Intraocular Pressure, Right Eye
entries.description: The fluid pressure inside the right eye (OD), measured by tonometry in mmHg. A normal reading is roughly 10-21 mmHg; higher pressures are a glaucoma risk factor. It is tracked separately from the left eye.

entries.name: Intraocular Pressure, Left Eye
entries.description: The fluid pressure inside the left eye (OS), measured by tonometry in mmHg. A normal reading is roughly 10-21 mmHg; higher pressures are a glaucoma risk factor. It is tracked separately from the right eye.

entries.name: Visual Acuity
entries.description: A measure of how clearly you can see, written as a Snellen fraction such as 20/20 (or 6/6 in metric). It is a qualitative value with no numeric reference range, so it is shown as a dated timeline rather than a chart. This entry is used when the report does not say which eye.

entries.name: Visual Acuity, Right Eye
entries.description: How clearly the right eye (OD) sees, written as a Snellen fraction such as 20/20. A qualitative value with no numeric reference range; it is shown as a dated timeline and tracked separately from the left eye.

entries.name: Visual Acuity, Left Eye
entries.description: How clearly the left eye (OS) sees, written as a Snellen fraction such as 20/20. A qualitative value with no numeric reference range; it is shown as a dated timeline and tracked separately from the right eye.

entries.name: PHQ-9
entries.description: A validated 9-item questionnaire that scores depression severity from 0 to 27, banded minimal / mild / moderate / moderately severe / severe. It is a screening instrument, not a diagnosis. Tracked here as a numeric score over time; informational, not medical advice.

entries.name: GAD-7
entries.description: A validated 7-item questionnaire that scores anxiety severity from 0 to 21, banded minimal / mild / moderate / severe. It is a screening instrument, not a diagnosis. Tracked here as a numeric score over time; informational, not medical advice.

entries.name: Periodontal Probing Depth
entries.description: The depth of the gum sulcus or periodontal pocket around a tooth, measured in millimeters with a periodontal probe (usually the deepest or a representative site). A healthy sulcus is about 1-3 mm; readings of 4 mm and deeper indicate periodontal pocketing worth monitoring. Lower is better; this is informational, not a diagnosis.

entries.name: Bleeding on Probing
entries.description: The proportion of periodontal sites that bleed when gently probed, reported as a full-mouth percentage. A full-mouth score under about 10% is consistent with periodontal stability; higher percentages suggest active gum inflammation. Lower is better; informational, not a diagnosis.

entries.name: Clinical Attachment Loss
entries.description: The distance from the cemento-enamel junction to the base of the periodontal pocket, in millimeters - the cumulative amount of tooth-supporting attachment that has been lost. Roughly 1-2 mm is mild, 3-4 mm moderate, and 5 mm or more severe. It tracks periodontitis progression over time; lower is better.
